<think>
Okay, I'm trying to figure out whether the mutant code change at line 135 of DateTimeZone.java is causing any issues. The original code had a condition that checked if iFixedOffsetCache was null and then initialized it. The mutant changed this by adding an if (false) block before initializing the cache.

First, I look at what the code does. The fixedOffsetZone method uses a static synchronized block to manage a cache of DateTimeZone instances. When the offset isn't zero, it checks if iFixedOffsetCache is null and initializes it if necessary. Then, it looks up or creates a new zone based on the ID.

The mutant change adds an if (false) condition around the initialization code. So, the cache will never be initialized because false is always false. This means that any subsequent calls to fixedOffsetZone won't have the cache set up, leading to potential issues where zones aren't cached properly or are created unnecessarily every time.

Looking at the failing tests information, several tests like TestDateTimeZone#testForOffsetHours_int and others started failing after this mutation. These tests probably rely on the cache being correctly initialized. For example, without the cache, each call might create a new zone instead of reusing one, leading to incorrect behavior in time calculations or comparisons.

The test that was fixed (TestMonthDay_Properties#testPropertyGetMonthOfYear) likely didn't depend directly on the cache but other mutants may have affected it. However, most tests are failing because they now encounter issues like NullPointerExceptions when methods return null due to the uninitialized cache.

So, the mutant change is causing a lot of tests to fail by preventing the cache from being set up correctly. This indicates that the mutant is not just changing behavior but breaking essential functionality, making it a faulty mutation.
</think>

The mutant code changes at line 135 prevent the `iFixedOffsetCache` from being initialized because the condition `if (false)` always evaluates to false. This leads to various tests failing due to the cache not being properly set up.
